title: Claude opus 4.6 + 4.7 on EURUSD, 60 days live. Win rate 64% TP1 / 57% TP2 / 43% TP3 at 1.1R / 1.7R / 2.5R with dynamic strategies in real time. Yes, it can trade...
author: The_AI_Trader
selftext:
quick follow up to my last post since a bunch of you asked specifically about EURUSD. ran the system live for 60 days, 183 sessions, 14 trades. 64% TP1 at 1.1R, 57% TP2 at 1.7R, 43% TP3 at 2.5R.

the part i actually want to talk about is how the macro and trend agents feed into the EURUSD-specific trader. macro agent runs the desk view. DXY direction, session bias, what oil/gold/VIX are saying, what came out of the london/NY handoff. trend agent runs pure structure on EURUSD itself, the CMT array, VWAP behavior across sessions, where the session high/low is sitting relative to the prior day. neither one calls the trade. they hand a packaged read to the EURUSD trader and the trader builds the thesis from scratch each session.

strategies aren't hardcoded. the trader builds the play based on what the agents handed it, volatility, liquidity, momentum structure, session conditions. so in theory every trade should look different. in practice there's a pattern and it's been bugging me.

across the 14 trades it keeps converging on three setups. pullback into trend after VWAP reclaim. fade the session high into prior-day resistance. mean reversion off session support when macro reads flat. not exotic, textbook desk patterns. brilliant in the sense that a seasoned trader would nod at all three. but i didn't tell it to prefer them. it found them on its own from a much wider menu of plays it could have built. i keep going back to the journals trying to figure out if it's converging because those three actually fit EURUSD's character at this volatility regime, or because something in the agent handoff is biasing it toward mean-reversion-flavored structure. honestly don't know yet.

the other thing i'm sitting with is the rejection rate. every session the agents produce roughly 1.8 setups on average, so across 183 sessions that's somewhere around 330 candidate trades the system built and monitored. only 14 made it through to execution. that's a 96% rejection rate against considered setups, or about 92% against sessions. that's not the trader being lazy, it's the trader being patient. it builds the thesis, watches conditions evolve in real time, and waits for the confluences to actually line up. if they don't, the setup expires. control like that was the thing i wasn't sure an LLM could hold session after session.

not going to dump the full conflict resolution logic in this post for length, but the short version is the trend agent sees the macro agent's read before it builds its own. one-way visibility. trader sees both and gets to weight. early data says the asymmetry matters more than i expected. ask away if you want to get into how the model logic is built inside.

4.7 only has 2 trades on it so i'm not calling that a comparison. ask me at session 300.

This is AI trading agents trading dynamically , so it’s a new world…

comments.author: Jehuty64
comments.body: Nice job. How does it trade ?

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
Thank you!

The app itself, has built in 6 core AI Traders, powered by Claude or GPT latest frontier models. As the post says, they work in a multiagentic manner with other agents such as the Macro, and Trend agents, with a huge array of data points in realtime, in order to intraday correctly. The problem with hooking up directly to an LLM, is that they lack the real time data to correctly identify trades with a statistical edge over time. Plus, trading is not a one man's job, it requires Macro Analisis, Technical Analisis, Entry Analisis, Risk analisis, and others. This platform leverages AI to mimick a full trading desk, and puts the user in the shoes of a fund manager, which is to assign risk accross your various AI Traders. And also monitors the trade, and decides in real time if the entry is valid or not. It's amazing to see how it rejects 90-95% of the setups it creates, as it monitors the entry zones in realtime, to decide to enter or not. It doesn't just produce the possible setup, it monitors and waits for a valid entry. T

as far as how it trades directly, the app has a couple of ways:

1. regular alert , just sends you an in app alert so you know if there is an entry. Within that entry, you can read and see the entire analisis, with the output of the main agent, and the other agents, so you can better understand the thought process, the trade, and other great insights. And you can also interact with the model, ask additional questions about that trade, for trade managemnet (once you are in the trade) and others. Perfect for manual traders.

2. webhook , you setup to send the webhook wherever you need it. 

3. direct connection to your broker, via our SkyTrading Bridge, cTrader and MT5 (mt5 coming soon). The bridge is a windows app, which you download in your computer. Then connect it to our SkyAutomated Trader, which is a bot that reads the signals and executes the trades on your account. 

  
So in essence, you can trade manually, use the webhook for your own process, or use the in-house trading bridge and automate internally with the use of your own computer, or a VPS (recommended, very stable) . 

The platform is called SkyAnalyst AI . You can look it up, and read more about it. This app is meant to bring true AI Trading Agents to retail traders. It tracks their performnace in real time. And allows you to also create your own trading agents, with your particular strategies. And it self tracks your agents performance, and the platform's built in agents as well. 

So you decide how to use the traders, diversify and leverage risk. 

Hopefully this answers your question. If you have any other, ask away!

comments.author: Dipluz
comments.body: Whats your net profit vs token cost ?

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
I spend around 400-500 dollars per month in token cost to run the built in 12 AI traders, their macro and trend agents, and ai entry evaluators. Each AI trader, has it's own team. So it's definitely not cheap.

However, I do trade large account, so it's net profitable for me. It also has a subscription model which helps with costs, and allows me to keep growing the platform. I personally believe trading agentic teams is the future of retail trading. 

Until this point, retail traders are forced to trade a handful of strategies, with 1-3 instruments tops. It's just hard to trade. Then you have to keep track, do individual analisis, keep your phsycology in check, have a bad ass risk management routine , and so on, so on, so on, so on....

With trading agents, you get 100x more diversification, cause you literally have the agents producing 1800-2000 setups per month, and the agents monitor and decide which trades to take or not. These 12 agents produce around 50-80 trades per month. The core 6 produce 25-40 trades per month. 

I can't think of how a single human could potentially replicate this. 

And then, what I propose, is now putting the retail trader in the role of a fund manager. So our job, is to look at all of our traders, analize their performance, and then just assign risk accross your traders. Like a full trading desk would . In small, medium, large hedgefunds, there is always a hierarchy above the traders on the floor. And the traders, are usually supported by analisis, risk, and other teams to help them trade better. They themselves, sometimes don't even know how much of the fund they manage. The managers decide where to put the higher risk to , how to leverage across different traders, etc. 

So the aim of SkyAnalyst AI , is to really put the trader into a new role, and instead of directly trading themselves, now they manage a team of agents with self tracking capability. 

That's definitely the dream, and so far, it's working. And I'm aware that 3-4 months of data (100+ trades in and growing ) is not a large data set... But considering it has over 12 agents, on 6 markets, deploying strategies dynamically , I think the results so far are highly indicative of the models performance....

And once Mythos is released.... and future models, the model will only get stronger and stronger. 

Hope this helps!

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
And one thing I want to clarify:

AI TRADERS ARE NOT CRYSTAL BALLS. 

However, they can be trained (as our data shows) to replicate professional traders, which have statistical edges ( they are called edges for a reason, usually a small advantage, but enough to make trading profitable) . 

That's the vision I have for retail trading.

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
Funny you mentioned DeepSeek v4. I'm actually running an AI Trading Benchmark site to compare the models performance, using my platform and agentic architecture. We are doing GPT 5.5 and Opus 4.7. But I'm very curious to see how the cheaper chinese models would do in that test. You just reignited that curiousity. 

Great to hear about your models results. I've been running it on live accounts for 3-4 months now. And the results mimic the results of professional succesful traders. It ain't no crystal ball, sorry folks, not SkyNet yet (maybe Mythos when it's released ?lol), but it has proven to give statistical edge , and has shown profitabiilty at professional levels. 

I'm running it on the API directly, so June 15 is not an issue for me. I already pay big bucks to run the model on 12 instruments ( 6 AI traders on OPUS and 6 more on GPT, 6 instruments, but they count as 12, since they all run their individual agentic team flows) . I spend around 400-500 a month on these base counts (hence why I'm curioius to see how Deepseek would fare) . BUt I do have users which make the development aspect profitable, and helps us keep growing the platform to bigger and higher heights. 

As far as your last comment, we track a very high selective rate. The models produce around 1400-1600 setups a month, which they only actually trade 3-5% . Imagine the patience it would require a human to have that discipline... LMAO!

Hopefully I answered your questions. Let me know more about your model! Sounds interesting!

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
Last thing, the models we have, do strategies dynamically. We don't give the models instructions on particular strategies (although some users get very specific on strategies, I favor letting the models think ), but I do give them a very strict process. Here's an example of a prompt the model is given to analyze. 

"Analyze EURUSD for NY AM session trade setups using these steps:

1. **Macro regime:** Read the Macro Analysis Agent bias, confidence, and factors. Check DXY 5-day data — if DXY is above its 5-day EMA and rising, bias bearish; if below and falling, bias bullish. Confirm with 10Y Treasury 5-day trend (rising yields = bearish EURUSD) and VIX direction (rising VIX = bearish EURUSD). DXY is the dominant driver. Hard rule: if Macro Agent confidence is above 70 and DXY trend aligns, do not take trades against that direction regardless of technicals. If Macro confidence is below 50, treat as range-bound — only trade at session extremes.
2. **Agent alignment:** Read the Trend Authority Agent direction, confidence, regime, key levels, and invalidation. If both Macro and Trend agents agree directionally with confidence above 60 each, this is the strongest foundation. If they diverge, drop confidence by 2 points and only consider mean-reversion trades at session high/low or VWAP.
3. **HTF bias:** From the 5 sixty-minute candles, check EMA stack (9>21>50 = bullish, inverse = bearish), RSI trend, and MACD histogram direction. Locate price relative to daily reference levels (open, high, low, S/R) and Trend Agent key levels.
4. **Calendar gate:** Check the economic calendar for high-impact USD or EUR events. Do not build entries within 15 minutes of high-impact events or within 5 minutes of the 8:30 or 10:00 AM data windows. If a high-impact event is imminent, wait for the post-release reversal to settle (at least 10 minutes after the release) before analyzing entry.
5. **LTF entry:** From the 5 fifteen-minute candles, confirm bias via EMA crossovers and RSI position (above 50 for longs, below 50 for shorts, not extreme). From the 10 five-minute candles, identify entry zones at VWAP tests, session high/low reactions, or Fibonacci level confluences with EMA support/resistance. Between 9:30-9:45 AM, watch for false breakouts of the pre-market range — only trust breakouts that hold past 9:45 as support/resistance.
6. **Confluence scoring:** Count from: (a) Macro Agent bias aligned, confidence >= 60, (b) Trend Agent aligned, confidence >= 60, (c) DXY 5-day trend confirms direction, (d) 10Y yield trend supports (rising = short, falling = long), (e) 60min EMA stack supports direction, (f) price at VWAP, session level, or Fibonacci on 5min, (g) 15min RSI confirms without extreme, (h) no high-impact event within 30 minutes. Require 5 of 8 for a setup. Rate: 5 = Medium (5.5-6.5), 6 = Medium-High (7), 7 = High (7.5-8), 8 = Very High (9+).
7. **Build setup:** If 5+ confluences, provide: directional bias, entry zone, entry trigger, stop loss beyond Trend Agent invalidation level or next structural level (minimum 1x 60min ATR from entry). Stop placement is structural, scaled to current volatility: on compressed days (VIX declining, narrow ranges), stops tighter near structure; on expanding days (VIX rising, wide ranges), stops wider to survive noise — but the setup must still meet minimum 1.5:1 R:R after the wider stop. If volatility makes R:R unworkable at structural stop levels, No Trade. If the structural stop exceeds the Trend Agent invalidation level, skip the setup. Add a small buffer beyond the stop for execution slippage — setups are forwarded directly to an automated trading system. TP1 should target 1R–1.25R at a structural level (Fibonacci, daily S/R, Trend Agent key levels). If no structure exists in that zone, evaluate the full target profile — a close TP1 with a strong TP2 at 2R+ is a valid trade. Reject only when the trade is structurally inverted: the highest-probability exit delivers less than 1R and reaching further targets requires breaking through major levels. Provide 2-3 take profit levels. If fewer than 5 confluences, state "no high-probability setup" with the specific confluences that failed.

Only output setups rated Medium confidence or higher. List confluences and risks for every setup."

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body: Each prompt, we refine it to the specific instrument being traded. Our internal prompts are crafted with AI agent teams, we run internally. So we try to do the research on the prompts as well. Hopefully after we have 1-2 years of data, we can refine these even more, and get them to produce better results.

replies.author: MycoHost01
replies.body:
wow thanks for sharing all this info with me! im also going the similar way with not giving it a strategy to follow but i have not given them what you mentioned here " but I do give them a very strict process. Here's an example of a prompt the model is given to analyze."

 they do have some gates and or guards but not to this level of detail they dont over trade  but theyll set a profit target above a cluster/liquidation zone and it just never gets to target majority of the time. it gets direction right but majority of the time it never hits the profit target because it sets it to high from a liquidation zone theres still quite alot i have to learn again thanks for sharing the info! am at 50 million plus tokens in total and only wasted 8 bucks since starting the highest day was 11 cents without the thinking feature!

 am not an expert on this stuff i just found it very interesting and dove right in.  it has had good wins but unfortunatly the losses still overweigh the wins but majority of those losses were right in direction is just that the target was set to high and it always gets like .50 to .80 cents close to target before it reverses. still really freaking interesting.  its analysis are on point its just the execution that am having problems with also deepseek just chose to leave its discount prices permanent this opened up alot of ideas for me to try and not worry about cost. 

  
again thanks for sharing this info!

comments.author: EfficientJeweler1430
comments.body: The 96% rejection rate is actually the most interesting part here are u logging the rejected setups with the same detail as the executed ones? Macro read, trend read, entry trigger, rejection reason, and what happened after rejection! with only 14 executed trades the rejected setup dataset might be more useful for validating whether the agents are genuinely filtering risk or just being overly selective

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
That's actually funny you pointed this out. Cause I thought the same thing. 

Originally, I was deleting the information of the trades after 10 days, to keep the database lean. But then , I said, holy cow, after 1-2 years, we have so much data that with stronger models in the future, this could be very very powerful. 

So we are keeping all the logs of rejected setups, validation history, etc. For a later more powerful model, to do analysis and looks on way to improve the model refinements and training. 

Greath thoughts!

comments.author: Robes_pr
comments.body: Can you give more informations please? For example: which trader platform you use and how you connected it, max dd, % gain, if we can use this bot and how

replies.author: The_AI_Trader
replies.body:
Hi Robes! Sure, let me go ahead and give you some of the rundown. 

1. It's called SkyAnlayst AI. You can look it up. It comes with 6 main AI traders, and you can create your own as well. It's a very robust platform built around the idea of agentic flows and agentic trading, leveraging the latest  Claude and GPT models. 

2. Drawdowns and gains are independent to each of the traders risk setup. However, we document every trade the model takes, and each AI trader has its own system to track it's performance. We publish this information daily on our website. And we have the stats updated in realtime, so you can see how our core AI traders are doing, how many trades they have taken, which trades, etc. How some models are doing better with specific instruments. For example, on our best AI Traders is with EURUSD. But with XAUUSD, it couldn't be profitable. So bad, we had to pull it. The model tends to fare well with SP500 and NAS100, but jury still out on US30. It's profitablitiy on the US30 is slightly positive. So we track in realtime. Also if you build your own AI trader, the system automatically tracks the trader for you. 

3. The system connects to cTrader, and soon to MT5. The only thing you need to watch for, is the price difference in brokerages for US Indexes. Forex, is stable. But brokers seem to vary with US30, and NAS100 specifiacallly. 

4. As far as how the system goes, I'm going to repost a reply for a question, so you can understand how the model works. 

Let me know if you have any questions. Love to help!

"Ok. Here it goes.

1. I built a backend with Supabase, that handles the generation of indicator data :  
a. RSI, MACD, FIBS, ATR, Sesions HIGH LOW (asia, london, US) , VWAP, volume,  
b. This is built across 60min, 15min, 5min. In essence replicating Trading View, and what a professional day trader would use to do day trading analysis.

c. It also goes and gives a 5 day picture of prices of GOLD, 10YR yield US, DXY, OIL, NY ADD (plus minus on stocks) , and a 5 day EMA above or below

d. All this data is organized in JSON blocks, properly fenced, and certain stuff like golden - death crosses, are sent in text, as well with values, so the LLM's can understand it better

e. Also a scraper , that gets economic calendar data and results, and sends this as part of the array

2. I built two additional AGENTS.

a. Macro agent goes with perplexity, and analyzes using an in depth prompt, the daytime macro perspective for the particular instruments. Does this every 30 min, and after any big announcment.

b. Trend agent, that basically gets all of the data for the last 5 hours on the first step, and gets the macro agent, and then outputs what it thinks about the instrument current trend (choppy, bull-bear, how strong, transitioning, macro supported or not)

3. So all of this output is fed into the main model, around 50k token data, and is queried with a very sequential way to analyze the data, and produce trade setups. Once it produces the setups, the model filters, and looks only for setups that are aligned with the trend, and have a certain score.

4. Once the model produces the trade setups, and the price goes into the ENTRY ZONE (which the model produces) then a final model is queried with now the decision SHOULD WE ENTER? Do you see favorable conditions to enter the trade? Is the entry requirmenets filled ? (these conditions are given by the original analysis, and the model is also free to decide if it sees a favorable entry). This step filters almost 95% of the trade setups. The AI is quite conservative.

5. This process is done every 30 minutes for 2 hours, from 9-11am EST. And it's currently being done to 6 markets . EURUSD , US30, NAS100, SP500, USDJPY, GBPUSD.

The AI does very well with EURUSD, NAS100, SP500. It is lightly profitable with US30. It's inconsistnetn with USDJPY (although I think the new refinnements, will make it produce better). GBPUSD is brand new, jury still out. And it sucked wind with XAUUSD, which I pulled it out this month. Tried a lot of stuff, couldn't make it trade it well . Even with GPT 5.5, and Opus 4.7. nothing."
